eli3: Mutual information measures how much knowing one variable reduces uncertainty about another. I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y). If X and Y are independent, knowing Y tells you nothing about X, so I(X;Y) = 0.
Equivalently: I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y). It's symmetric: I(X;Y) = I(Y;X). Think of it as the amount of information shared between X and Y — how much knowing one helps you predict the other.
MI relates to KL divergence: I(X;Y) = KL(P(X,Y) || P(X)P(Y)). So it's zero exactly when X and Y are independent (joint equals product of marginals).

whenWhy.title: Feature selection
whenWhy.body: Select features X with high MI with target Y: I(X;Y). Captures any relationship (linear or nonlinear), unlike correlation. Fast MI-based selection (MIM, mRMR) scales to high dimensions. Used in bioinformatics, text classification.

whenWhy.title: Information bottleneck method
whenWhy.body: Tradeoff between compression and prediction: minimize I(X;T) (bottleneck representation T) subject to I(T;Y) ≥ threshold. Used for interpretability, fair representation learning, and deep learning theory.

whenWhy.title: Clustering evaluation
whenWhy.body: Normalized mutual information (NMI) compares cluster assignments to true labels. Adjusted for chance (AMI). Alternative to purity, Rand index. Captures information-theoretic agreement between partitions.

whenWhy.title: Representation learning
whenWhy.body: Maximize MI between representations and original data (InfoNCE, Deep InfoMax). Contrastive learning (SimCLR, MoCo) maximizes MI between augmented views of same image. Used in self-supervised learning.

deepDiveIntro: Mutual information between X and Y: I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y) = H(Y) - H(Y|X) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y). Symmetric, non-negative, I(X;Y) = 0 iff X ⟂ Y. Alternative form: I(X;Y) = Σ_{x,y} p(x,y) log(p(x,y)/(p(x)p(y))) = D(P(X,Y)||P(X)P(Y)) — KL divergence from independence.

Conditional mutual information: I(X;Y|Z) = H(X|Z) - H(X|Y,Z). Chain rule: I(X₁,…,Xₙ;Y) = Σᵢ I(Xᵢ;Y|X₁,…,Xᵢ₋₁). Data processing inequality: if X → Z → Y form a Markov chain, then I(X;Z) ≥ I(X;Y) — processing can't increase information.

deepDive.heading: Definitions and Properties
deepDive.body: I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y) = H(Y) - H(Y|X) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y). Symmetric: I(X;Y) = I(Y;X). Non-negative: I(X;Y) ≥ 0. Zero iff independence. Bounded: I(X;Y) ≤ min(H(X), H(Y)).

deepDive.heading: Conditional Mutual Information
deepDive.body: I(X;Y|Z) = H(X|Z) - H(X|Y,Z). Information between X and Y given Z. Measures conditional dependence. Chain rule: I(X,Y;Z) = I(X;Z) + I(Y;Z|X). Used in conditional independence testing, causal discovery.
deepDive.formula: I(X;Y|Z) = Σ p(z) I(X;Y|Z=z) = H(X|Z) - H(X|Y,Z)

deepDive.heading: Data Processing Inequality
deepDive.body: If X → Z → Y (Markov chain), then I(X;Z) ≥ I(X;Y). No processing of Z can increase information about X. Foundation of information theory. Equality iff Z is a sufficient statistic: X → Z → Y and I(X;Z) = I(X;Y) implies Y adds no info beyond Z.

deepDive.heading: Estimating MI from Data
deepDive.body: Hard problem: need density estimation. Methods: (1) Histogram/binning (biased, doesn't scale). (2) k-NN estimators (Kraskov-Stögbauer-Grassberger). (3) Neural estimators (MINE: DV representation, InfoNCE). (4) Kernel density estimation. Each has tradeoffs.

deepDive.heading: Normalized Variants
deepDive.body: Raw MI depends on marginal entropies. Normalized versions for comparison: NMI = 2I(X;Y)/(H(X)+H(Y)) or I(X;Y)/√(H(X)H(Y)). Range [0,1]. Adjusted MI corrects for chance (similar to adjusted Rand index).

youtubeLabel: Mutual Information — Machine Learning Street Talk
takeaways: MI I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y): information shared between X and Y.
Data processing inequality: I(X;Z) ≥ I(X;Y) for X→Z→Y.
Hard to estimate from data; methods include k-NN and neural estimators.

edgeCases.q: Can MI be larger than individual entropies?
edgeCases.a: No. I(X;Y) ≤ min(H(X), H(Y)). The maximum MI is the smaller of the two entropies, achieved when one variable is a deterministic function of the other. If X = Y, then I(X;Y) = H(X) = H(Y).

edgeCases.q: Why is estimating MI from samples hard?
edgeCases.a: MI requires joint density p(x,y), which is hard to estimate in high dimensions. Histogram/binning suffers from curse of dimensionality. k-NN methods are better but still struggle. Neural methods (MINE) are promising but can be unstable. This is the fundamental challenge in information bottleneck and representation learning.

edgeCases.q: What's the relationship between correlation and MI?
edgeCases.a: For jointly Gaussian variables, I(X;Y) = -½ log(1-ρ²) — one-to-one mapping. But MI captures any dependence (nonlinear, non-monotonic), while correlation only captures linear relationship. Variables can have zero correlation but high MI (e.g., X and X² for symmetric distributions).

sources.label: Cover & Thomas — Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed.), Ch. 2
Kraskov et al. — Estimating Mutual Information
